metadata.subject: Science
metadata.source: Red Square Exam Intensive – MCS P.5 Science
metadata.note: Questions that require diagrams or pictures to answer have been omitted.

questions.text: It takes about 24 hours to complete one ___ of the Earth.
It takes the Earth about (a) ___ to complete one rotation. It is bright on the side facing the Sun, so it is (b) ___.
The Earth rotates from (a) ___ to (b) ___.
The imaginary axis of the Earth passes through the North Pole and the South Pole.
There are different seasons because the Earth is rotating.
Countries in the west receive daylight before countries in the east.

Identify the features of the Earth's rotation and revolution. (There can be more than one answer.) (1) Rotation  (2) Revolution. Features: A. It takes one year to complete.  B. It takes one day to complete.  C. It takes one month to complete.  D. The Earth rotates anti-clockwise on its axis.  E. The Earth revolves clockwise around the Sun.  F. The Earth revolves anti-clockwise around the Sun.
questions.answer: Rotation: B, D.  Revolution: A, F.

questions.text: How long does it take for the Earth to complete one rotation?
Explain why the Sun always rises in the east and sets in the west.
questions.answer: The Earth rotates from west to east (anti-clockwise when viewed from the North Pole). As it rotates, the eastern parts of the Earth face the Sun first, so the Sun appears to rise in the east. As the Earth continues rotating, the Sun appears to move westward across the sky and eventually set in the west.

questions.text: The Earth moves around the Sun once in a year. This is called ___.
The Earth moves around the Sun once in a (a) ___. This is called (b) ___.
Since the Earth's rotational axis is (a) ___, the angles of (b) ___ shone over the Southern and Northern hemispheres are different.
When the Northern Hemisphere receives (a) ___ sunlight, the temperature is higher. When it receives (b) ___ sunlight, the temperature is lower.
The (a) ___ axis allows the Northern Hemisphere to receive stronger sunlight at different times. Therefore, we experience different (b) ___ each year.
The angles of sunlight over the Northern and Southern Hemisphere are always different.
Australia is in the Northern Hemisphere.
When the sunlight shines directly on the Northern Hemisphere, it is summer in the Southern Hemisphere.
Why are there different seasons?
questions.answer: There are different seasons because the Earth's axis is tilted. As the Earth revolves around the Sun, the tilted axis causes different hemispheres to receive sunlight at different angles throughout the year. When a hemisphere tilts toward the Sun, it receives more direct sunlight and has higher temperatures (summer). When it tilts away from the Sun, it receives more slanted sunlight and has lower temperatures (winter).

questions.text: Tom studies in Australia. Which hemisphere is Australia in?
Which season is it in Australia in December? What kind of sunlight does Australia receive at that time?
questions.answer: It is summer in Australia in December. Australia receives direct / overhead sunlight.

questions.text: Suggest ONE activity that Tom can do during the Australian summer in December.
questions.answer: Any suitable outdoor summer activity, e.g. swimming / going to the beach / playing outside / having a picnic.

questions.text: Tom is in Australia in December. His father is in London. Which season is it in London in December? Explain.
questions.answer: It is winter in London in December. London is in the Northern Hemisphere. In December, the Northern Hemisphere tilts away from the Sun, so it receives more slanted / indirect sunlight. This means less heat is received, resulting in lower temperatures and winter.

questions.text: The Earth is divided into (a) ___ time zones. Therefore, there are time (b) ___ among different places on the Earth.
The Earth is divided into (a) ___ time zones. Therefore, there is a time (b) ___ among different places on the Earth.
People divide the Earth into different time zones for convenience purposes.
The time difference between one time zone and the next is either one or half hour.
Hong Kong and Beijing are in the same time zone.
China covers several time zones on the map.
If a map shows north at the top, in which direction is Seoul from Hong Kong?
questions.answer: North (Seoul is to the north of Hong Kong)

questions.text: A plane takes off from Hong Kong to Seoul at 12:00 noon. The flight takes 3.5 hours. The screen shows an estimated arrival time of 4:30 p.m. Is the arrival time shown correct? Why or why not?
questions.answer: Yes, the arrival time is correct. Seoul is east of Hong Kong and is 1 hour ahead of Hong Kong time. 12:00 noon + 3.5 hours = 3:30 p.m. Hong Kong time = 4:30 p.m. Seoul time. So 4:30 p.m. is the correct local arrival time in Seoul.

questions.text: A plane flies from Hong Kong to Seoul and arrives 1 hour later than expected due to the time zone difference. How is this phenomenon related to the rotation of the Earth?
questions.answer: The Earth rotates from west to east. Countries that are further east receive sunlight earlier, so their clocks are set to an earlier (ahead) time. Seoul is to the east of Hong Kong, so Seoul is 1 hour ahead of Hong Kong. This time difference exists because of the Earth's rotation — as the Earth rotates, different parts of the Earth face the Sun at different times.

questions.text: Tom is in Australia on 3rd December at 7:00 a.m. Australia is 10 hours ahead of London. What is the date and time in London at that moment?
questions.answer: 2nd December, 9:00 p.m.  (7:00 a.m. minus 10 hours = 9:00 p.m. the previous day)

questions.text: The Moon ___ light from the Sun.
It takes about ___ days to complete one lunar cycle.
The Moon has a rugged surface with a lot of ___.
The moonlight we see is the light ___ from the Sun.
The Moon rotates anticlockwise.

Match the following to the correct lunar day. (1) On the 1st day of a lunar month  (2) On the 15th day of a lunar month. Descriptions: A. Cannot see the Moon in the sky.  B. A full moon in the sky.  C. The Moon shapes like an oval.  D. The fully lit side of the Moon faces the Earth.  E. The dark side of the Moon faces the Earth.  F. The Sun is between the Moon and the Earth.
questions.answer: (1) 1st day: A, E  (2) 15th day: B, D

questions.text: Why can we see different phases of the Moon?
questions.answer: As the Moon revolves around the Earth, we see different portions of its lit side (the side facing the Sun) from Earth. When different amounts of the lit side face us, we see different shapes (phases) of the Moon.

questions.text: Why do we always see the same side of the Moon?
questions.answer: The Moon takes the same amount of time to rotate on its own axis as it takes to revolve around the Earth (approximately one month for each). Because these two periods are equal, the same side of the Moon always faces the Earth.

questions.text: When can we see a full moon?
questions.answer: On the 15th day of the lunar month, when the fully lit side of the Moon faces the Earth.

questions.text: In a solar eclipse model where the students' heads represent the Earth, what do the clay and the foam ball represent?
questions.answer: The clay represents the Moon. The foam ball represents the Sun.

questions.text: When does a solar eclipse occur?
questions.answer: A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon moves between the Earth and the Sun, blocking the sunlight from reaching the Earth.

questions.text: When does a solar eclipse occur?
questions.answer: A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon is between the Earth and the Sun, blocking sunlight from reaching part of the Earth.

questions.text: When does a lunar eclipse happen?
questions.answer: A lunar eclipse happens when the Earth is between the Sun and the Moon, and the Earth's shadow falls on the Moon.

questions.text: Is it safe to observe a solar eclipse directly with our eyes? Why?
questions.answer: No, it is not safe. The Sun emits very intense light and radiation that can burn the retina and cause permanent eye damage or blindness.

questions.text: It is safe to watch a solar eclipse with a pair of sunglasses.
Suggest one correct way to observe a solar eclipse.
questions.answer: Use a pinhole projector (pinhole camera) to project the image onto a surface / use special solar eclipse glasses / watch it on television.

questions.text: Apart from using a pinhole projector, suggest another safe way to watch a solar eclipse.
questions.answer: Use special solar eclipse glasses / watch it on television / use a mirror to project the image onto a wall.

questions.text: Why should we not look at the Sun directly?
questions.answer: The Sun emits very intense light and radiation. Looking at it directly can burn our retina and cause permanent eye damage or blindness.
